content:
A quick roundup of the biggest cybersecurity stories, breaches, threats, and developments from 22/06 to 28/06 — everything you need to know in a few minutes.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo de Milad Fakuriansur UnsplashGitHub patches known vulnerability and launches hardening program
On June 24 2026, GitHub announced a major security update aimed at mitigating “pwn request” attacks. These attacks exploit the pull_request_target trigger when combined with unsafe configurations, allowing malicious code from external forks to execute with full workflow privileges and access sensitive secrets (tokens, API keys, credentials). With the new release, GitHub now blocks these attack patterns by default, only allowing them if developers explicitly enable an override option. The secure-by-default settings are scheduled to be automatically rolled out to supported major versions starting July 16, 2026, applying broadly across repositories unless workflows are pinned to specific versions.
This change follows a series of concrete security incidents linked to this vulnerability. In May 2026, attackers exploited a pwn request and compromised around 170 packets. GitHub acknowledged that the issue has been known for several years and stems primarily from misuse rather than a flaw in the trigger itself, and previously focused on user awareness rather than patching the vulnerability.
Despite the new protections, risks remain for other workflow events, and GitHub indicated further hardening measures may follow. The update is part of a wider response to the increasing volume of supply chain attacks in open-source environments, including earlier incidents where attackers exfiltrated source code from approximately 3,800 internal GitHub repositories.
Changes in Europe’s cybersecurity landscape
Germany is accelerating its strategic shift toward technological and operational autonomy in defense and cybersecurity, amid rising geopolitical tensions and digital threats. Berlin is preparing a €580 million contract with defense startup Helsing to build the core software architecture of a national “combat cloud” system, linking aircraft, drones, satellites and sensors into a unified warfare network. This move follows the effective collapse of the joint Franco-German-Spanish Future Combat Air System (FCAS) fighter jet program, with Germany now prioritizing a sovereign, software-centric approach seen as the backbone of future air combat. This decision echoes France’s homeland security’s (DGSI) recent choice to replace Palantir, which provided data analysis services, by French provider Chapvision, in an effort to limit dependence on foreign corporations to handle national intelligence. At the same time, the German government is planning new “active cyberdefence” powers, enabling authorities to directly disrupt or disable hostile infrastructure, as cybercrime surges, reaching hundreds of thousands of cases and causing over €200 billion in damages annually.
At the European level, security governance is also in transition, with the race underway to appoint the next head of Europol, the EU’s law enforcement agency. Three senior official, Jürgen Ebner, Alicia Malo and Linas Pernavas, have been shortlisted to succeed Catherine De Bolle. The new head of the agency will have to face growing scrutiny over Europol’s expanding digital policing powers, which have repeatedly brought it into conflict with the EU’s data protection watchdog over how it collects and manages sensitive data. The appointment also comes as Europol’s mandate expands in response to mounting hybrid threats, cybercrime and cross-border criminal networks.
Russia shifts into high gear
Russia-linked cyber and intelligence operations are intensifying across multiple fronts, blending traditional espionage, cyberattacks and digital surveillance tools. A recent investigation by CitizenLab and the FBI found that Russian authorities used Cellebrite forensic technology (months after the company had formally halted sales to Russia) to unlock the phone of jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov, extracting private communications and contacts to support a political prosecution. Separately, ukrainian authorities, in coordination with the FBI, have uncovered large‑scale social engineering campaigns using fake “support” messages to hijack messaging accounts of officials and activists across Ukraine, Europe and the United States.
At the same time, Russia-linked hacking groups are sustaining and expanding cyber operations tied to the war in Ukraine. The Gamaredon group, attributed to Russian intelligence, has ramped up spear‑phishing campaigns against Ukrainian government and military targets. These campaigns rely more heavily on legitimate cloud and online services to mask infrastructure and evade detection, reflecting a shift toward more stealthy and scalable attack methods.
Western agencies warn that these operations extend well beyond Ukraine. The FBI and its partners have exposed Russian military intelligence hacking networks that compromised thousands of routers worldwide, allowing attackers to redirect internet traffic, harvest credentials and conduct covert surveillance against government, military and critical infrastructure targets.
The new “Mythos Era”
The cybersecurity landscape is entering what experts describe as a “Mythos Era,” from Anthropic’s new AI, able to detect software vulnerabilities with unprecedented speed and accuracy. Google warns that hackers have already used AI to discover and exploit zero‑day vulnerabilities, demonstrating that machines can uncover subtle logic errors in software that humans and automated tools miss. Western intelligence agencies now caution this shift will accelerate sharply, with “frontier” AI models expected to supercharge cyberattacks within months rather than years, lowering barriers for malicious actors while increasing the speed, scale and sophistication of operations.
This disruptive potential is compounded by growing geopolitical tensions around advanced AI systems. Anthropic has accused Chinese corporation Alibaba of orchestrating a large‑scale “distillation” campaign to extract capabilities from its Claude model through millions of interactions, highlighting fears of industrial‑scale AI capability theft. Meanwhile Mythos has already demonstrated the ability to identify vulnerabilities in highly classified U.S. government networks within hours during controlled tests, underscoring both its defensive value and its potential misuse.
Cyber vocab of the Week : WORM
Write Once Read Many (WORM) storage is a data protection model that ensures information becomes immutable as soon as it is written: it can be read indefinitely but can no longer be modified, overwritten, or deleted. This immutability is enforced at the storage level (hardware or software), which rejects any attempt to alter existing data (even by highly privileged administrators) often until a predefined retention period expires. As a result, WORM guarantees a reliable “source of truth,” preserving data integrity against accidental changes, insider threats, or cyberattacks.
WORM plays a critical role in cybersecurity and compliance, particularly as a safeguard against ransomware. By making backup data technically unchangeable, it prevents attackers from encrypting, deleting, or tampering with recovery points, ensuring organizations can restore systems after an incident. Beyond security, it is also widely used in regulated industries (finance or healthcare) to meet strict requirements for data retention, auditability, and traceability, providing verifiable evidence that records have not been altered over time.

I am a junior cybersecurity professionnal and CISSP Associate, with experience in IT Audit and Cybersecurity Consulting. My areas of interest include cyber resilience (especially CNI security), OT cybersecurity and data protection.
